supplementEntry.name: Glutathione
supplementEntry.evidenceTierRationale: Multiple randomized controlled trials demonstrate oral and liposomal glutathione can raise body GSH stores and improve immune markers (Richie 2015 RCT n=54; Sinha 2018 pilot n=12). However, one well-designed RCT (Allen & Bradley 2011 n=40) found no effect on oxidative stress biomarkers. Bioavailability remains debated; liposomal forms show stronger results than standard oral. Evidence is mixed and mostly from small trials, warranting Tier 2 until large multi-center replication.

cognitive: Neuroprotective antioxidant defense in the brain
nonCognitive: Master antioxidant replenishment and oxidative stress reduction
Immune function enhancement (NK cell activity, lymphocyte proliferation)

supplementEntry.optimalDuration: 4 weeks to 6 months (GSH store elevation observed as early as 1-2 weeks with liposomal forms)
mechanismsOfAction: Direct scavenging of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reactive nitrogen species (RNS)
Glutathione peroxidase (GPx) cofactor for hydrogen peroxide and lipid peroxide detoxification
Phase II detoxification via glutathione S-transferase (GST) conjugation reactions
Maintenance of thiol redox balance (GSH/GSSG ratio) in cells
Regeneration of other antioxidants including vitamins C and E
Regulation of immune cell proliferation and NK cell cytotoxicity
Inhibition of tyrosinase activity reducing melanin synthesis in skin
Mitochondrial membrane integrity protection against oxidative damage

contraindications: Organ transplant recipients on immunosuppressants (theoretical immune modulation concern)
drugInteractions: May reduce efficacy of chemotherapy agents that rely on oxidative mechanisms
Potential interaction with nitroglycerin (enhanced hypotensive effect)
May affect acetaminophen metabolism via glutathione conjugation

effectSizes.erythrocyteGsh1000mg: 30-35% increase in erythrocyte, plasma, and lymphocyte GSH at 6 months (p<0.05, RCT n=54)
effectSizes.buccalCellGsh1000mg: 260% increase in buccal cell GSH at 6 months (p<0.05, RCT n=54)
effectSizes.nkCellCytotoxicity1000mg: >2-fold increase in NK cell cytotoxicity at 3 months vs placebo (RCT n=54)
effectSizes.liposomalWholeBloodGsh: 40% increase in whole blood GSH after 2 weeks (pilot n=12, liposomal form)
effectSizes.liposomalNkActivity: Up to 400% increase in NK cell cytotoxicity at 2 weeks (pilot n=12, liposomal form)
effectSizes.hba1cReduction: Significant HbA1c improvement vs placebo after 6 months in elderly T2D patients (RCT)
effectSizes.skinMelaninReduction: Consistent melanin index decrease at multiple body sites vs placebo (RCT n=60, 500 mg/day, 4 weeks)

qualityMarkers: Liposomal delivery verified by third party

keyCitations.title: Randomized controlled trial of oral glutathione supplementation on body stores of glutathione
Oral supplementation with liposomal glutathione elevates body stores of glutathione and markers of immune function
Effects of oral glutathione supplementation on systemic oxidative stress biomarkers in human volunteers
keyCitations.journal: Journal of Alternative and Complementary Medicine

keyCitations.title: Effects of N-acetylcysteine, oral glutathione (GSH) and a novel sublingual form of GSH on oxidative stress markers: A comparative crossover study
Randomized Clinical Trial of How Long-Term Glutathione Supplementation Offers Protection from Oxidative Damage and Improves HbA1c in Elderly Type 2 Diabetic Patients
Glutathione as an oral whitening agent: a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study
Deficient synthesis of glutathione underlies oxidative stress in aging and can be corrected by dietary cysteine and glycine supplementation
Application of Glutathione as Anti-Oxidative and Anti-Aging Drugs

mechanisms.mechanism: Direct ROS scavenging and glutathione peroxidase cofactor activity

evidence.title: Randomized controlled trial of oral glutathione supplementation on body stores of glutathione
evidence.findings: Oral GSH at 1000 mg/day increased erythrocyte, plasma, and lymphocyte GSH levels by 30-35% and buccal cell GSH by 260% at 6 months. The 250 mg/day dose produced 17-29% increases. Oxidative stress markers (8-OHdG) decreased in both dose groups.
evidence.methodology: Double-blind RCT with 250 mg/day, 1000 mg/day, and placebo arms over 6 months

evidence.title: Application of Glutathione as Anti-Oxidative and Anti-Aging Drugs
evidence.findings: Comprehensive review establishing GSH as the primary intracellular thiol antioxidant. Decreased GSH levels are associated with aging and a wide range of pathological conditions including neurodegenerative disorders. GSH functions as cofactor for GPx enzymes and directly scavenges ROS.
evidence.methodology: Narrative review of glutathione biochemistry and clinical applications

mechanisms.mechanism: Enhanced bioavailability via liposomal delivery bypassing GI degradation

evidence.title: Oral supplementation with liposomal glutathione elevates body stores of glutathione and markers of immune function
evidence.findings: Liposomal GSH at 500 and 1000 mg/day elevated GSH within 1 week. Maximum increases at 2 weeks: 40% in whole blood, 25% in erythrocytes, 28% in plasma, 100% in PBMCs. Faster onset than standard oral GSH in Richie 2015 trial.
evidence.methodology: Open-label pilot study with two dose levels, assessments at 1, 2, and 4 weeks

evidence.title: Effects of N-acetylcysteine, oral glutathione (GSH) and a novel sublingual form of GSH on oxidative stress markers: A comparative crossover study
evidence.findings: Sublingual GSH produced significantly higher plasma GSH levels and GSH/GSSG ratio (p=0.003) compared to standard oral GSH. Demonstrated significant superiority of sublingual delivery over oral form for both bioavailability and oxidative stress reduction.
evidence.methodology: Randomized three-arm crossover comparing sublingual GSH, oral GSH, and NAC over 21 days each

mechanisms.mechanism: Phase II detoxification via glutathione S-transferase conjugation

evidence.title: Application of Glutathione as Anti-Oxidative and Anti-Aging Drugs
evidence.findings: GSH serves as essential co-substrate for glutathione S-transferase (GST) enzymes catalyzing Phase II conjugation of electrophilic xenobiotics, carcinogens, and drug metabolites. GSH depletion impairs detoxification capacity and increases susceptibility to toxic insult.
evidence.methodology: Narrative review of glutathione biochemistry

mechanisms.mechanism: Immune cell regulation via thiol redox modulation

evidence.title: Glutathione and immune function
evidence.findings: Intracellular GSH levels regulate T lymphocyte function and proliferation. Even moderate GSH depletion impairs immune cell activation. Cysteine supply is rate-limiting for immune cell GSH synthesis, particularly under conditions of oxidative stress or infection.
evidence.methodology: Review of glutathione-immune system interactions

evidence.title: Oral supplementation with liposomal glutathione elevates body stores of glutathione and markers of immune function
evidence.findings: Liposomal GSH elevated NK cell cytotoxicity by up to 400% at 2 weeks and lymphocyte proliferation by up to 60% at 2 weeks. Demonstrates functional immune enhancement from GSH supplementation.
evidence.methodology: Open-label pilot study with serial immune function assessments

benefits.specificClaim: Oral glutathione supplementation increases body GSH stores and reduces oxidative stress markers
benefits.replicationStatus: Conflicting results: Richie 2015 positive (n=54), Allen & Bradley 2011 negative (n=40). Dose, duration, and formulation differences may explain discrepancy.

evidence.title: Randomized controlled trial of oral glutathione supplementation on body stores of glutathione
evidence.findings: 1000 mg/day oral GSH increased erythrocyte, plasma, and lymphocyte GSH by 30-35% and buccal cell GSH by 260% at 6 months. 250 mg/day also effective (17-29% increases). Oxidative stress marker 8-OHdG decreased in both groups.
evidence.methodology: Double-blind RCT with two dose arms and placebo over 6 months

evidence.title: Effects of oral glutathione supplementation on systemic oxidative stress biomarkers in human volunteers
evidence.journal: Journal of Alternative and Complementary Medicine
evidence.findings: 500 mg twice daily oral GSH for 4 weeks produced no significant changes in biomarkers of oxidative stress, lipid peroxidation, DNA adduct formation, or glutathione status in healthy adults. Suggests limited bioavailability of standard oral GSH in short-duration studies.
evidence.methodology: Double-blind RCT with 1000 mg/day oral GSH vs placebo for 4 weeks

benefits.specificClaim: Glutathione supplementation enhances NK cell cytotoxicity and lymphocyte function
benefits.replicationStatus: Consistent across Richie 2015 RCT and Sinha 2018 pilot; liposomal form shows stronger immune effects

evidence.title: Randomized controlled trial of oral glutathione supplementation on body stores of glutathione
evidence.findings: NK cell cytotoxicity more than doubled in the 1000 mg/day group compared to placebo at 3 months. First RCT evidence linking oral GSH supplementation to functional immune enhancement.
evidence.methodology: Double-blind RCT with immune function as secondary endpoint

evidence.title: Oral supplementation with liposomal glutathione elevates body stores of glutathione and markers of immune function
evidence.findings: Liposomal GSH elevated NK cell cytotoxicity by up to 400% and lymphocyte proliferation by up to 60% within 2 weeks. More rapid immune enhancement than standard oral GSH observed in Richie 2015.
evidence.methodology: Open-label pilot study with serial assessments at weeks 1, 2, and 4

benefits.specificClaim: Long-term glutathione supplementation improves HbA1c and reduces oxidative damage in type 2 diabetes

evidence.title: Randomized Clinical Trial of How Long-Term Glutathione Supplementation Offers Protection from Oxidative Damage and Improves HbA1c in Elderly Type 2 Diabetic Patients
evidence.findings: 6 months oral GSH supplementation (500 mg/day) significantly improved HbA1c, reduced oxidative damage markers (MDA, protein carbonyls), and increased blood GSH levels compared to placebo in elderly T2D patients.
evidence.methodology: Randomized, placebo-controlled trial with 6-month follow-up in elderly T2D patients

benefits.specificClaim: Oral glutathione reduces melanin synthesis and lightens skin
benefits.replicationStatus: Multiple small RCTs with consistent direction of effect, but short duration and variable magnitude

evidence.title: Glutathione as an oral whitening agent: a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study
evidence.findings: 500 mg/day oral GSH consistently decreased melanin indices at all six measured body sites. Statistically significant reductions at two sites compared to placebo. Authors noted long-term safety not yet established.
evidence.methodology: Double-blind RCT with melanin index measurements at six anatomical sites

benefits.specificClaim: Glutathione depletion underlies age-related oxidative stress and can be corrected with precursor supplementation
benefits.replicationStatus: Consistent findings from Sekhar 2011 and broader aging literature

evidence.title: Deficient synthesis of glutathione underlies oxidative stress in aging and can be corrected by dietary cysteine and glycine supplementation
evidence.findings: Elderly subjects had markedly lower glycine and cysteine levels and reduced GSH synthesis. 2 weeks of cysteine and glycine supplementation increased GSH concentration by approximately 95% in elderly subjects, restoring levels to match young adults and significantly reducing oxidative stress markers.
evidence.methodology: Clinical trial with isotope tracer methodology measuring in vivo GSH synthesis rates

safety.safetyAspect: General tolerability of oral glutathione supplementation
safety.claim: Oral glutathione is well-tolerated with only mild gastrointestinal side effects across multiple clinical trials up to 6 months

evidence.title: Randomized controlled trial of oral glutathione supplementation on body stores of glutathione
evidence.findings: No serious adverse events reported at either 250 mg/day or 1000 mg/day over 6 months. Mild side effects included flatulence (n=5), loose stools, flushing (n=2), and weight gain (n=1). No clinically significant changes in hematology, hepatic, or renal function tests.
evidence.methodology: Double-blind RCT with comprehensive safety monitoring including blood panels

evidence.title: Effects of oral glutathione supplementation on systemic oxidative stress biomarkers in human volunteers
evidence.journal: Journal of Alternative and Complementary Medicine
evidence.findings: 1000 mg/day oral GSH for 4 weeks was well-tolerated with minimal side effects and >90% participant adherence. No serious adverse events reported.
evidence.methodology: Double-blind RCT with safety and adherence monitoring

safety.claim: Liposomal glutathione is safe and well-tolerated in healthy adults at doses up to 1000 mg/day

evidence.title: Oral supplementation with liposomal glutathione elevates body stores of glutathione and markers of immune function
evidence.findings: Liposomal GSH at 500 mg/day and 1000 mg/day was safe and well-tolerated for 4 weeks. No adverse events reported in the study.
evidence.methodology: Open-label pilot study with safety monitoring

safety.claim: Sublingual and liposomal GSH show superior bioavailability compared to standard oral capsules; standard oral GSH may have limited absorption

evidence.title: Effects of N-acetylcysteine, oral glutathione (GSH) and a novel sublingual form of GSH on oxidative stress markers: A comparative crossover study
evidence.findings: Sublingual GSH showed significantly higher plasma GSH levels and GSH/GSSG ratio (p=0.003) vs oral GSH. Establishes that delivery form substantially affects glutathione bioavailability and clinical outcomes.
evidence.methodology: Three-arm randomized crossover comparing sublingual GSH, oral GSH, and NAC

dosage.claim: Both 250 mg/day and 1000 mg/day oral GSH increase body stores, with 1000 mg/day showing larger effect sizes

evidence.title: Randomized controlled trial of oral glutathione supplementation on body stores of glutathione
evidence.findings: Dose-response observed: 250 mg/day increased blood GSH by 17-29%, while 1000 mg/day increased by 30-35% in blood compartments and 260% in buccal cells. NK cell activity doubled at 1000 mg/day. Both doses effective but higher dose showed greater benefit.
evidence.methodology: Double-blind RCT with two dose arms and placebo

dosage.claim: Liposomal delivery achieves clinically meaningful GSH elevation within 1-2 weeks

evidence.title: Oral supplementation with liposomal glutathione elevates body stores of glutathione and markers of immune function
evidence.findings: Liposomal GSH at 500 and 1000 mg/day elevated whole blood GSH by 40%, plasma GSH by 28%, and PBMC GSH by 100% within 2 weeks. Maximum immune function enhancement (400% NK cell activity) also at 2 weeks.
evidence.methodology: Open-label pilot with serial assessments
